title: Beeswax shoe polish
subtitle: A traditional beeswax and jojoba shoe polish for smooth leather shoes
excerpt: Beeswax shoe polish provides shine, water resistance, and leather conditioning in one tin. Jojoba penetrates the leather and conditions it before the beeswax surface layer sets. Cedarwood essential oil adds the traditional leathery-woody fragrance. This is a natural alternative to commercial paste polishes.
sourceNotes: Beeswax shoe polish formulation from Traditional Leatherworking, Waterer (1981). Jojoba penetration properties from supplier datasheet. Cedarwood EO from Tisserand Institute (tisserandinstitute.org, May 2026).

recipe.yieldDescription: 1 small tin (~75 g) of shoe polish
recipe.batchNotes: Scale proportionally. Pour into small tins: 50 to 100 g per tin is the usual size for home use.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: The polish keeps for 2 years and makes an excellent gift with a cloth or horsehair brush included.
recipe.temperatureNote: Melt beeswax over a water bath to around 65 degrees Celsius. Remove from heat before adding jojoba and essential oil to prevent loss of the fragrance top notes.
recipe.shelfLifeNotes: 2 years in a sealed tin. No water content means no preservative is required. The polish may soften slightly in summer: this is normal and does not affect performance. Store in a cool place.

recipeTools.notes: Or any heat-proof jug; dedicated to non-food use

glossaryTerms.definition: The process of generating a high shine on leather by rubbing vigorously with a soft cloth after the polish has dried. The friction generates slight heat, which causes the wax to flow into micro-scratches on the surface and then reset as a level, reflective layer. A horsehair brush is traditional; a soft cotton cloth works equally well.

content.text: Traditional shoe polish consists of wax, oil, and solvent. This formula replaces the solvent with jojoba, which conditions the leather while the beeswax builds the surface layer needed for

. The absence of a petroleum solvent makes this formula slower to apply: work a thin layer in with a cloth and allow it to penetrate for 5 minutes before buffing. Two thin layers give a better result than one thick one.

Test on a hidden patch first. Beeswax polish may darken light-coloured leathers slightly, particularly unfinished or antiqued leathers. On standard smooth black or dark brown leather this is not noticeable. Do not use on suede, nubuck, or patent leather.

items.prepNote: Cosmetic-grade; provides the polish and protective layer
Conditioning oil; penetrates the leather before the wax sets

content.text: Melt the beeswax in the jug over a simmering-water bath. When fully liquid, remove from heat. Stir in the jojoba oil and then the cedarwood essential oil. Pour into a small tin. Leave to set at room temperature undisturbed for 1 hour.

To use: clean shoes to remove surface dirt. Apply a small amount of polish with a cloth using circular movements. Leave for 5 minutes to penetrate. Buff vigorously with a horsehair brush or a clean soft cloth until a shine develops.
2 years in a sealed tin. Store away from heat sources. The polish will soften in warm conditions but firm up again when cool.
